text: The video demonstrates a laparoscopic D2 gastrectomy in an elderly patient. The surgeon begins with a total omentectomy, starting near the mid portion of the transverse colon and progressing towards the splenic flexure, freeing the omentum on the left side. Attention is then turned to the right side, performing omentectomy towards the hepatic flexure. The lesser sac is opened, dividing attachments between the pancreas and stomach, revealing the pancreas. Dissection continues close to the head of the pancreas, pylorus, and D1 junction, working near the duodenum. The gastrocolic vessel and right gastroepiploic vessel are clipped and divided using an energy source and cold scissors. A small window is created towards the lesser curvature, carefully working near the gastroduodenal artery. Hepatoduodenal nodal dissection is performed, exposing the common bile duct and common hepatic duct, with lymphovascular tissue removed.

The surgeon continues dissection from the anterior aspect of the common hepatic duct, revealing the hepatic artery below the instrument. A bluish hue indicates the portal vein, which is becoming more visible. The right and left hepatic arteries are seen running towards the liver, with the right gastric artery clipped and cut. Lymphovascular tissue surrounding these structures is removed, leaving vessels exposed. A bulky node at the celiac axis is excised, and the hiatus is dissected, removing fatty tissue around it. The duodenum is divided 2.5 to 3 cm below, exposing the gastroduodenal artery and its pulsation, along with the prehepatic portion of the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon holds the tissues, revealing the splenic artery beneath the instrument. The coronary vein is ligated at its origin using two proximal clips and one distal clip, then divided with cold scissors. The left gastric artery is visible, surrounded by lymphovascular tissue that is removed. The aorta comes into view, and dissection is performed carefully in this high-pressure zone using three proximal clips and an energy source. The lymphovascular tissue over the aorta is taken down, and dissection continues towards the short gastric vessels near the pancreas tip. The spleen and stomach are visible, and a comprehensive DII lymphadenectomy is performed, ensuring all lymphovascular tissue is removed.

The surgeon performs a subtotal gastrectomy, avoiding splenic hilar dissection. The gastroduodenal artery, branches of the hepatic artery, and portal vein are clearly visible. Clips are applied to the right gastric artery, and the celiac axis area is exposed. The duodenum is transected, and fatty tissue on the lesser curvature side is dissected, leaving the area bare. The splenic short gastric vessels are identified, running towards the cranial end, while other vessels run towards the caudal end. A green cartridge is used to staple the thick stomach tissue, crossing the previous staple line.

The surgeon ensures the staple line is secure before using cold scissors to divide between the four rows of staples, completing the division of the stomach. The gastrocolic vessel is clearly visible, confirming the correct surgical plane. The jejunum is measured at approximately forty centimeters from the DJ flexure, and a small enterotomy is made. A posterior gastrotomy is performed using ultrasonic shears, maintaining a distance of three centimeters from the staple line to ensure vascular integrity for the anastomosis. The stapler is introduced, and tissues are pulled together before stapling. A stay suture is placed on the inferior aspect, and suturing begins from the superior aspect, ensuring a comprehensive closure with a one-centimeter overlap beyond the enterotomy.

The surgeon uses a PDS suture to close the enterotomies, starting from the superior aspect. Minimal mucosa is taken on the jejunum side, while a full-thickness bite is made on the stomach portion. The needle is precisely placed to ensure a secure anastomosis. A rails tube is inserted through the gastrojejunostomy (GJ) for posterior wall protection and potential nutritional support. A four-centimeter vertical incision is made in the upper midline to remove the specimen, which includes the omentum and stomach. The jejunojejunostomy is performed extracorporeally.
